data.title: Marsh expects 2026 underlying revenue growth similar to 2025 while targeting $400M Thrive savings
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Marsh & McLennan Companies (MRSH) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “Overall, we grew revenue 8% in the quarter. Underlying revenue increased 4% despite lower fiduciary interest income and continued downward pricing pressure in insurance and reinsurance,” said CEO John Doyle, adding: “Adjusted operating income grew 8% from a year ago, and adjusted EPS also grew 8%.”
  • Doyle outlined executive changes tied to execution and growth, saying “Mark was named Chief Operating Officer of Marsh in addition to serving as our CFO,” and “We also announced Nick as the CEO of Marsh Risk,” while “Martin South… is now our Chief Client Officer” and “Ted succeeded Nick as CEO of Marsh Management Consulting.”
  • Doyle framed AI as central to strategy and margins, stating “Our strategy leverages our scale and capacity to invest in AI,” across “3 main pillars… growth… productivity… efficiency,” and said AI-driven automation and BCS initiatives are “building our confidence in continued margin improvement.”
  • CFO/COO Mark McGivney said, “Consolidated revenue increased 8% to $7.6 billion with underlying growth of 4%,” and added: “GAAP EPS was $2.36 and adjusted EPS was $3.29, up 8% over last year.”

Outlook

  • Doyle reiterated the full-year setup despite headwinds: “We continue to expect underlying revenue growth in 2026 to be similar to last year. We also anticipate continued margin expansion and solid adjusted EPS growth.”
  • McGivney provided key modeling items for the next quarter, saying, “Looking ahead to the second quarter, we expect fiduciary interest income will be approximately $80 million,” and “we anticipate corporate expense of approximately $90 million, which includes some one-off timing items.”
  • On the pace of margin expansion, McGivney said, “For modeling purposes, we expect to generate more margin expansion in the second half of this year than in the first half.”

Financial results

  • McGivney reported profitability and margin levels: “Operating income was $1.8 billion and adjusted operating income was $2.4 billion, up 8%. Our adjusted operating margin was unchanged at 31.8%.”
  • Segment performance included Risk & Insurance Services revenue of “$5.1 billion, up 6%… or 3% on an underlying basis,” with Marsh Risk revenue “$3.7 billion, up 8%… or 4% on an underlying basis,” and Guy Carpenter revenue “$1.2 billion, up 3% or 2% on an underlying basis.”
  • Consulting segment results included “first quarter revenue… $2.6 billion, up 11% or 5% on an underlying basis,” and Mercer revenue “$1.7 billion… up 11% or 5% on an underlying basis,” while Marsh Management Consulting revenue was “$897 million… up 10% or 6% on an underlying basis.”
  • Noteworthy items included litigation: “Noteworthy items this quarter also include a $425 million charge relating to litigation stemming from the collapse of Greensill Capital in 2021,” McGivney said, adding, “The charge in the quarter represents the best estimate of our liability in this case.”

Q&A

  • Charles Peters, Raymond James: asked about margin expansion durability and “risks of AI disintermediation”; CEO John Doyle responded, “we've guided to year 19 of margin expansion this year, and we fully expect to do that,” and added, “we've competed with early-stage tech-enabled start-ups for a long time… and competed successfully.”
  • Peters, Raymond James: asked about buybacks vs. M&A given broker stock resets; Doyle said, “we want a balanced approach to capital management,” and highlighted “$750 million of buybacks in the first quarter,” while noting the pending acquisition: “We announced the acquisition of AltamarCAM… with about $20 billion of AUM.”
  • Michael Zaremski, BMO: asked about claims advocacy value-add and AI; President & CEO of Marsh Risk Nicholas Studer said, “Claims IQ… we've got several thousand colleagues now drawing on AI-enabled analysis of almost $200 billion of loss information,” and described upcoming launches: “the first applications powered by the Risk Cortex… are going to be launched in couple of weeks at RIMS.”
  • Brian Meredith, UBS: asked if AI productivity gains will be retained or competed away; Doyle said, “Our fees have been for a long time, stable as a percentage of premium,” and added, “if you think we're a discount insurance broker… but we're not.”
  • David Motemaden, Evercore ISI: asked for AI spend/partners and exposure to less-complex personal lines; Doyle said, “we've not shared that data in the past,” and added personal lines is “almost entirely a high net worth personalized client… It's not a material part of our business.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical and valuation-focused, pressing on AI disruption, margin sustainability, and capital allocation, including questions on “risks of AI disintermediation” (Peters, Raymond James) and whether AI gains will be “competed away” (Meredith, UBS).
  • Management tone was confident in prepared remarks and firm in Q&A, with Doyle using emphatic language such as “we fully expect to do that” on margin expansion and “we expect to be an AI winner,” while declining specificity on AI budget: “we've not shared that data in the past.”
  • Versus last quarter, the Q1 call leaned more heavily into AI execution detail (tools, automation examples, new “Risk Cortex” applications) and introduced leadership changes, while maintaining the same macro framing around pricing pressure and “headwinds from lower interest rates.”

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 introduced executive committee changes (McGivney adding COO; Studer leading Marsh Risk; South as Chief Client Officer; Moynihan leading Marsh Management Consulting) that were not described as such in Q4.
  • Guidance language remained consistent quarter to quarter, with Doyle repeating in both calls that 2026 underlying revenue growth is expected to be “similar to last year,” alongside “continued margin expansion.”
  • Market commentary stayed focused on softening pricing: Q1 cited “primary commercial insurance rates decreased 5% in Q1” following “a 4% decline in the fourth quarter of 2025,” and described reinsurance rate reductions (including “down 15% to 20%” for certain accounts) as a growth headwind but client benefit.
  • Q1 added a significant litigation-related item (the “$425 million charge” tied to Greensill), which was not referenced in Q4 prepared remarks.

Risks and concerns

  • Management cited “continued downward pricing pressure in insurance and reinsurance” and “headwinds from lower interest rates,” while pointing to mitigation via “strong sales,” Thrive/BCS efficiency, and AI-enabled productivity.
  • McGivney flagged ongoing legal uncertainty, stating on Greensill-related litigation, “this litigation is ongoing, so we aren't able to comment further at this time.”
  • Doyle cautioned that the “economic and geopolitical environment could change materially from our assumptions,” and discussed potential global economic uncertainty if Middle East conflict becomes “a sustained conflict in the region.”

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as “a solid start in 2026,” reiterating expectations for 2026 underlying revenue growth “similar to last year,” continued margin expansion, and “solid adjusted EPS growth,” while highlighting leadership changes intended to “accelerate pace” and a detailed AI roadmap tied to growth, productivity, and efficiency. The quarter also included a disclosed “$425 million charge” related to Greensill litigation, alongside ongoing capital deployment plans and continued emphasis on balancing acquisitions with share repurchases depending on the M&A pipeline.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: The world is seeing a global inflation cycle upturn beyond heightened oil prices – Economic Cycle Research Institute
data.description:
The “window of vulnerability on growth is not open,” according to Lakshman Achuthan, co-founder and COO at the Economic Cycle Research Institute, who argues that a cyclical upturn in global inflation represents the defining economic shift of the current year.

In an interview with the Bloomberg Surveillance podcast, Achuthan emphasized that while many focus on headline-driven reactions, the underlying cyclical direction points clearly toward persistent inflationary pressures.

The inflation cycle has expanded well beyond U.S. borders, with future inflation gauges turning up “really cleanly” in Japan, Germany, and even China, where deflation fears had recently dominated.

Achuthan pointed to Japanese government bonds reaching a 29-year high and German bunds hitting a 15-year high as evidence of this global phenomenon.

“The global inflation cycle upturn, which preceded the war, is a thing,” he said, noting that rising commodity prices driven by the global industrial upturn began well before geopolitical conflicts emerged.

Achuthan expressed skepticism toward the market’s tendency for “knee-jerk reactions” and warned that the Federal Reserve may resurrect the word “transitory” if oil prices (CO1:COM), (CL1:COM) decline following any potential ceasefire.

“There’s a cyclical upturn. It goes much broader than simply oil prices,” he cautioned, suggesting that such labeling would miss the deeper structural forces at work.

Multiple factors are driving this persistent inflation, including bottlenecks in the system, supply chain disruptions, productivity that is “not exactly holding up,” and pent-up demand among higher-income consumers.

Achuthan noted that while there may be a “great longer-term story” around productivity gains from AI, those benefits are not materializing immediately enough to counter current inflationary pressures.

Market allocators hoping for an “inflationary boom” that could support equities may find themselves disappointed if inflation remains stubbornly elevated.

“That’s the beauty of cycles,” Achuthan explained regarding determining economic direction. “It’s on or off. And there’s a lot of noise. But what’s the underlying direction? Inflation is going to go higher.”

The Federal Reserve faces a challenging predicament as coincident data begins confirming the cyclical upturn, with core PCE exceeding three percent and core PPI approaching four percent.

Achuthan stressed that meaningful adjustments to risk outlook require examining both cyclical impulses and potential negative shocks rather than reacting to short-term headlines alone.

data.title: Via Transportation points to success with the Sioux Falls public transit project
data.description:
Via Transportation (VIA) updated in a LinkedIn post that the company has helped Sioux Falls restructure its public transit system so that nearly every resident can reach the whole city, including jobs, without additional budget increases. The city's Sioux Area Metro selected Via (VIA) as its technology and operations partner in late 2023, giving the company responsibility for day-to-day management, software, and route planning starting in 2024.

Via said it rebuilt Sioux Falls' network of 85 square miles around a combination of high-frequency fixed-route buses and citywide microtransit, extending coverage to 100% of the city and eliminating prior transit deserts in outlying neighborhoods. Notably, the system now uses data-driven routing to bundle riders into efficient shared trips while simplifying the bus lines so that core corridors run more often and with fewer long waits. Riders can book trips via an app that presents both on-demand microtransit and fixed-route options, making it easier to plan trips end-to-end.

The result is that Sioux Falls residents now have access to roughly 50K more jobs within a one-hour transit trip, nearly doubling overall job access compared with the old network, while lowering the cost per ride by about 25%.

Shares of Via Transportation (VIA) rose 1.4% in afternoon trading to $17.38. The transit tech company priced its IPO last September at $46 per share and traded as high as $56.31 before trending down.

data.title: MDU Resources started Neutral at J.P. Morgan as premium valuation offsets pipeline upside
data.description:
MDU Resources (MDU) trades little changed Thursday as J.P. Morgan initiated coverage with a Neutral rating and $22 price target, saying positive sales growth trends, regulatory diversification, and upside tied to favorable Bakken natural gas dynamics should help support growth durability, enhanced by successful execution of Bakken East.

However, JPM's Aidan Kelly said MDU's (MDU) current double-digit premium valuation largely reflects its growth runway, track record, and repositioning, leaving limited room for multiple expansion.

Under MDU's (MDU) base plan, Kelly sees a relatively narrow margin for execution against its targeted 6%-8% earnings per share compound annual growth rate, particularly when factoring in the spread between rate base growth and equity needs; that said, the analyst pointed to WBI Energy's pending Bakken East project standing out as a core lever that could lift realized EPS growth above the current range in 2029-30.

data.title: Stock market sentiment is reversing to pre-war tailwinds – Crossmark Global Investments
data.description:
The stock market’s rally to record highs reflects investors’ optimism that pre-war economic tailwinds will return as the US-Iran conflict appears to be winding down, according to Bob Doll, CEO, and CIO at Crossmark Global Investments.

“Prior to this war, the economy was pretty good. Earnings were better than pretty good. This war has been brief. And if it really is over or close to over, we’ll go back to those tailwinds, and that’s what the market is focusing on,” Doll said.

In an interview with Bloomberg Podcasts, Doll explained that the current market environment has created an unusual dynamic where nearly any news drives stocks higher.

“Why is this happening? Because good news is good news, bad news is good news, and no news is good news. You buy stocks,” he said.

The strategist also noted that the traditional investor base is shrinking relative to new market participants, including hedge funds, quantitative tools, and young individual investors who wield significant buying power.

Despite the rally to new all-time highs, Doll expressed caution about current valuations. He noted he would prefer a lower multiple than the current 21x earnings given the remaining uncertainty in the market.

While early earnings reports have been positive, he anticipates analysts’ estimates may need to come down as the season progresses, with many having hesitated to update their forecasts during the height of the conflict.

As geopolitical tensions ease, Doll expects themes like artificial intelligence (AIQ), (BOTZ) and private credit to return to the forefront of investor attention. He believes software stocks (IGV), (XSW) are “coming back from the dead” and that semiconductors (SMH), (SOXX), while undergoing a necessary correction, are not finished. However, he remains cautious about AI-related capital expenditure numbers, suggesting that expectations may need adjustment.

For investors seeking opportunities, Doll is currently favoring the financials sector (XLF), (VFH), (IYF).

“Look how much they’ve been pummeled since the first of the year. The numbers so far are pretty good. So, I would, on this weakness year to date, add to my financial position,” he explained. His investment strategy emphasizes price to free cash flow and high return on equity to ensure both quality and growth.

Looking at longer-term trends, Doll highlighted the importance of emerging market consumers for global economic growth, given shrinking populations in developed nations like China and Japan.

“If the planet’s going to grow, the emerging market consumer has to carry the day,” he said, describing it as a volatile but essential investment theme for the future.

U.S. markets tracking ETFs: (DIA), (DDM), (DOG), (DXD), (SDOW), (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (QQQ), (QQQM), (TQQQ), (QID), and (SQQQ).

data.title: What's next for Nike after a 15% post-earnings drop?
data.description:
Nike (NKE) shares experienced a sharp 15% decline following the release of its Q3 earnings report, despite the company beating top- and bottom-line estimates. Investors reacted negatively to forward guidance that predicts a 2% to 4% sales decline in fiscal Q4 and continued weakness in the critical China market, where revenue is forecasted to drop by 20%. At constant currency, revenues were down approximately 3% year-over-year, with particular weakness across EMEA and China.

Still, while the market’s reaction was swift and punishing, analyst sentiment remains split on whether the “Win Now” strategy and a return to wholesale roots can spark a long-term recovery.

What Do Seeking Alpha Analysts Say About Nike’s Future?

Bulls pointed to Nike’s pivot from a failed Direct-to-Consumer strategy back to wholesale distribution under new CEO Elliott Hill as a rare turnaround opportunity. They highlighted strong gross margins that held better than expected at 40.2%, aggressive inventory clearance efforts, and a sub-1.5x price-to-sales ratio—well below competitors like Deckers Outdoor (DECK)—that they believe offers a compelling margin of safety. With a 3.1% dividend yield and nearly 50% upside potential to a $68 target, bullish analysts see current levels near $46 as an attractive entry point.

Bears, however, highlighted the structural “footwear gap” of $1 billion compared to FY24 and deteriorating demand in EMEA and China. They pointed to a slow-moving turnaround and a valuation that remains difficult to justify at 29x trailing and forward earnings while volumes continue to fall. Management’s acknowledgment that cleanup efforts in China could weigh on revenue through fiscal 2027 reinforced concerns that being too early on this turnaround is the same as being wrong.

Here’s a breakdown of what some analysts had to say:

  • Elina Selianska, Rating: Buy: “Nike offers a rare turnaround opportunity as it pivots from a failed DTC strategy back to wholesale distribution under new CEO Elliott Hill… current levels near $52 provide a compelling margin of safety.” - NIKE: Return To The Old Strategy

  • Justin Purohit, Rating: Buy: “The headlines aren’t rosy, but I believe this presents an attractive opportunity for long-term positioning.” - Nike Q3: Turnaround Plan Turns Investors Away, Shares Attractive

  • IWA Research, Rating: Buy: “NKE is upgraded to Buy as the stock trades below intrinsic value… recovery will take time and is dependent on both internal execution and external factors.” - Nike: Turnaround Advancing As Macro Headwinds Create A Buying Opportunity (Upgrade)

  • Luca Socci, Rating: Hold: “Nike still trades at a premium while growth is missing, leaving the stock near fair value with limited upside and a turnaround that remains unproven.” - Why Nike Beat Earnings But Dropped 9% (Rating Upgrade)

  • Deep Value Investing, Rating: Hold: “China remains central to Nike’s earnings story, and management said cleanup efforts there could keep weighing on revenue through fiscal 2027… I’m staying on the sidelines.” - Nike: A Textbook Trap For Contrarians

  • Brian Gilmartin: "At $43 per share, Nike is close to trading at 1x TTM revenue, a metric that hasn’t been at this level probably since the 1990s, or during periods of significant duress like 2008 or the COVID lows. However, Nike is still 30x and 50x cash flow and free cash flow (ex-cash)." - Nike Earning Summary

What Do the Quant Ratings Say?

Nike currently carries a Hold rating from Seeking Alpha’s quant system, reflecting a tug-of-war between high-quality profitability and stalling growth metrics.

Profitability: The company continues to show resilience in its margins, earning a high grade as gross margins held better than expected at 40.2%, even as volumes decreased.

Valuation: This category has seen improvement, receiving a favorable grade as the price-to-sales ratio fell below 1.5x, which is significantly lower than competitors like Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK).

Growth: Growth remains a significant concern, receiving a poor grade due to forecasted sales declines of 2% to 4% for the upcoming quarter and a structural $1 billion gap in footwear revenue.

Momentum: Momentum is weak, earning a low grade following the 9% post-earnings drop and a 21% loss in share value over the past year.

Earnings Revisions: This metric remains under pressure as analysts adjust for management’s guidance of continued revenue declines through fiscal 2026.

What’s the Latest News on Nike?

Renewed insider interest has emerged in the stock after the footwear company’s shares fell nearly 30% year-to-date. Two executives recently made purchases—one by director Robert Swan and another by CEO Elliott Hill. Additionally, Tim Cook, CEO of Apple, also increased his stake in the sportswear giant.

The sportswear giant is reportedly in discussions to become the official match ball supplier for the UEFA Champions League through 2031, potentially replacing long-time partner Adidas (ADDYY)  (ADDDF).

data.title: KeyCorp signals at least $1.3B of 2026 buybacks while targeting ~3.05% net interest margin exit
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: KeyCorp (KEY) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “Our strong first quarter performance demonstrates disciplined execution and significant momentum as we continue to deliver on our commitments,” said (Chairman, President & CEO Christopher Gorman), highlighting “earnings of $0.44 per share,” “return on tangible common equity exceeded 13%,” and that the company remains on track for “15%-plus return on tangible common equity by year-end 2027.”
  • (CEO Gorman) tied profitability to balance-sheet trends, saying “net interest margin expanded 5 basis points sequentially to 2.87%” and the bank remains “on track to exceed 3% net interest margin by year-end,” while noting “commercial loan growth was strong and broad-based… increasing $3.3 billion or 4% sequentially.”
  • (CEO Gorman) emphasized capital return and regulation tailwinds: “we… repurchased nearly $400 million of common stock,” and “subject to market conditions, we expect to buy back at least $1.3 billion of our shares in 2026, up from the $1.2 billion we previously communicated,” while adding the revised Basel proposal could provide “a 100-plus basis point benefit to our marked CET1 ratio.”
  • (Chief Financial Officer Clark Khayat) summarized operating leverage and credit: “Revenue was up 10% year-over-year, while expenses increased by 4%,” and “loan loss provision of $106 million included… net charge-offs… and a reserve build of $5 million,” with the build reflecting “additional qualitative reserves to account for the macro uncertainty.”
  • (CFO Khayat) provided expanded exposure detail on private credit and NDFI, stating, “we estimate approximately $10.9 billion of outstandings as of March 31,” and adding, “through the first quarter, all of our facilities are performing as structured and required.”
  • (CEO Gorman) announced a leadership scope change: “Clark has assumed an expanded role to lead our technology and operations organization, in addition to his role as CFO.”

Outlook

  • (CFO Khayat) raised 2026 net interest income growth guidance: “We now expect full year net interest income growth of 9% to 10% compared to our prior guide of 8% to 10%.”
  • (CFO Khayat) updated margin and balance-sheet targets: “We now also expect to exit the year with a net interest margin of approximately 3.05% on a stable earning asset base relative to the first quarter,” and said the base case “assumes no cuts this year.”
  • (CFO Khayat) increased loan growth expectations: “Average loans are expected to increase 2% to 4% compared to our previous guidance of 1% to 2% and average commercial loans are now expected to grow 6% to 8% this year.”
  • (CFO Khayat) reiterated expense discipline despite investment: “We continue to feel very comfortable with our full year expense growth guide of 3% to 4%.”

Financial Results

  • (CEO Gorman) reported “revenue grew 10% year-over-year with revenue growing more than 2x the rate of expenses,” and (CFO Khayat) added “taxable-equivalent net interest income increased 11% year-over-year and was up 1% sequentially.”
  • (CFO Khayat) described fee drivers, including “investment banking and debt placement fees… $197 million,” and guided that “second quarter investment banking fees” are planned “in the $175 million to $180 million range,” while still being “very comfortable that investment banking fees will grow mid-single digits in 2026.”
  • (CFO Khayat) highlighted deposits and funding actions: “Average deposits decreased by 2% sequentially… and the intentional runoff of $1.6 billion in higher-cost brokered CDs,” while “total deposit costs declined… to 1.65%.”
  • (CFO Khayat) described credit metrics and one-quarter movement: “Net charge-offs were $101 million… an annualized 38 basis points,” and “nonperforming assets increased by $65 million… driven by 2 credits in utilities and multifamily real estate.”
  • (CFO Khayat) detailed capital positioning and buyback pacing: “Our CET1 ratio was 11.4% and our marked CET1 ratio was 10%,” and “we expect to repurchase at least $300 million of our shares per quarter for the balance of the year.”

Q&A

  • L. Erika Penala, UBS: Asked about client sentiment amid “geopolitical volatility” and private credit spreads; (Chairman, President & CEO Gorman) responded, “The consumer is in great shape,” and on private credit said spreads show “a firming,” adding, “these redemptions are real… [and] will give the banks… an opportunity to re-intermediate.”
  • Kenneth Usdin, Autonomous: Asked where deposits and NIB mix bottom; (Chief Financial Officer Khayat) said deposits should “trough kind of mid-May and then build up,” with “ending balances… higher” and costs likely “fairly stable” under the “no cuts” base case.
  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: Asked about more aggressive lending competition; (CEO Gorman) said “there's been just a constant pressure on spreads and on structure,” while (Chief Risk Officer Mohit Ramani) added, “we have not adjusted any of our credit boxes or underwriting standards.”
  • Ryan Nash, Goldman Sachs: Pressed on what drives loan growth from here; (CEO Gorman) said there is “appropriate conservatism” in guidance, citing “utilities and power,” “healthcare,” and “commercial real estate transactional activity,” while noting ongoing runoff of “$0.5 billion to $600 million of… residential mortgages per quarter.”
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Asked how excess capital would be deployed if Basel changes advance; (CFO Khayat) said $1.3B is expected to be “more like the floor,” but added, “I don't think anybody should expect one big swing at this.”
  • Michael Mayo, Wells Fargo: Challenged the mid-single-digit investment banking growth outlook; (CEO Gorman) said “that's what it is based on where we are right now,” and described middle-market deal caution as clients “slow playing it.”
  • Ebrahim Poonawala, BofA: Asked about AI data center exposure risk; (CEO Gorman) said funding is “in the capital markets and also at some of the banks,” while (CRO Ramani) added, “our data center exposure is fairly de minimis.”
  • Christopher O'Connell, KBW: Asked about higher-for-longer impacts on margin and private credit risk ranking; (CFO Khayat) said the margin guide “incorporates a flat no cut scenario,” and (CEO Gorman) said, “I wouldn't put private credit in my basket of things that we're really focused on right now.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on investment banking growth, capital deployment under Basel, and credit hot spots, including Michael Mayo’s characterization that “mid-single-digit growth… [is] not so exciting.”
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, using phrases such as (CEO Gorman) “I emphasize the word may” on a lending inflection and (CFO Khayat) “we'll play that by ear” on buyback upside beyond the $1.3B.
  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from broad confidence around 2026 momentum to more explicit macro caveats, including (CEO Gorman) “macroeconomic environment has continued to be dynamic” and added “qualitative loan loss reserves… to account for a wider range of potential macroeconomic outcomes.”

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance moved higher on core earnings drivers: Q1 raised 2026 net interest income growth to “9% to 10%” (from “8% to 10%” previously) and average loans to “2% to 4%” (from “1% to 2%”), while prior-quarter messaging emphasized a path to “manage… marked CET1… down… by the end of 2026.”
  • Capital return messaging increased: Q4 projected “at least $1.2 billion” in 2026 buybacks and Q1 increased that to “at least $1.3 billion,” alongside Q1’s note of nearly “$400 million” repurchased in the quarter.
  • Q1 placed heavier emphasis on NDFI/private credit disclosures and monitoring, including detailed investment-grade mix and “structural protections,” whereas Q4 discussed NDFI more at a portfolio level and referenced being willing to “stay on the sidelines” on deals.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from Q4’s broader strategy/Board and guidance conservatism toward Q1’s execution questions on deposits, loan competition, investment banking durability, and capital flexibility tied to Basel.

Risks and Concerns

  • (CEO Gorman) said buyback pace could be impacted “if we had a severe macroeconomic downturn and started having credit losses,” while adding, “We do not see that.”
  • (CFO Khayat) said the reserve build reflected “macro uncertainty,” and (CEO Gorman) added the bank “added to our already elevated qualitative loan loss reserves.”
  • Credit watch areas cited in Q&A included (CEO Gorman) “agriculture” and “transportation,” and he also flagged “oil and gas producers” exposure, while (CFO Khayat) emphasized operational and reputational risks in payments, calling it “a technology and software business” with heightened “fraud or security” focus.

Final Takeaway

Management described a quarter defined by higher profitability, modestly better margin, and strong commercial loan growth, paired with increased 2026 targets for net interest income growth, loan growth, and share repurchases. Executives emphasized disciplined underwriting, additional qualitative reserving for macro uncertainty, and expanded transparency on private credit/NDFI exposures, while also framing Basel III Endgame revisions as potential capital flexibility that could support continued buybacks and investment in technology, operations, and frontline hiring.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: White House looks to give agencies access to Anthropic's Mythos model: report
data.description:
Despite its battle with the Pentagon, Anthropic (ANTHRO) may get a boost from the White House as the Trump Administration looks to give federal agencies access to its Mythos model, Bloomberg reported.

Gregory Barbaccia, who is the federal Chief Information Officer at the Office of Management and Budget, told Cabinet departments that OMB is working on allowing their agencies to use Mythos, the news outlet reported, citing an internal memo. The email does not state when the agencies will get access or that they absolutely will, but it adds more information will be available “in the coming weeks.”

Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark said earlier this week that the company, which some investors have valued at $800B, was in discussions with the Trump administration about Mythos.

“We have a narrow contracting dispute, but I don't want ​that to get in the way of the fact that we ​care deeply about national security,” Clark said at the Semafor World Economy event in Washington, referring to the aforementioned issue with the Pentagon. “Our position is the government has to know ​about this stuff … So absolutely, we're talking to them about ​Mythos, and we'll talk to them about the next models as well.”

Anthropic and the White House did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

So far, Mythos, which was originally leaked last month, has been made available to some of the biggest companies in tech. It was made available in preview to roughly 50 companies and organizations that deal with critical internet infrastructure, including Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), and the Linux Foundation, according to previous reports.

Amazon and Google are among the investors in Anthropic.

Earlier this month, Anthropic unveiled Claude Mythos Preview, a general-purpose frontier model, which the company described as its “most capable yet for coding and agentic tasks.” 

“Its strength in cybersecurity is a direct result of that broader capability: a model that can deeply understand and modify complex software is also one that can find and fix its vulnerabilities,” Anthropic added.

Following the unveiling of Mythos, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell reportedly summoned Wall Street leaders to an urgent meeting on concerns that Mythos would usher in an era of greater cyber risk.

Financial leaders in the U.K. have also reportedly started to evaluate potential threats linked to the new AI model.

Separately, on Thursday, the American Securities Association said Mythos poses significant risks to individual investors through the Securities and Exchange Commission investor database.

data.title: Defense stocks break the usual war trade
data.description:
Defense shares (ITA) (XAR) have broken from their usual wartime playbook following U.S.-led Operation Epic Fury, with major contractors posting losses of roughly 10%–20% in the first 30 trading days since the Middle East conflict began at the end of February, according to a recent Arbor Data Science analysis.

The decline stands in sharp contrast to prior military escalations, where defense names typically rallied as investors priced in higher weapons demand and extended spending cycles.

Arbor’s comparison shows the sector posted solid gains following Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine, the Hamas attack on Israel in 2023, Russia’s 2014 annexation of Crimea, the post-9/11 war period and the 1990 Gulf War.

This time, General Dynamics (GD), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Northrop Grumman (NOC) and RTX (RTX) have all weakened early even as broader geopolitical risk stayed elevated.

The divergence marks an unusual moment for investors who have long relied on defense stocks as a hedge during military escalations.

data.title: Markets flip the switch from fear to FOMO, Citadel’s Rubner says
data.description:
A notable shift is underway in U.S. equity markets as investor positioning stabilizes and trading activity broadens, according to Citadel’s head of equity and equity derivatives strategy, Scott Rubner.

After a period marked by elevated volatility and defensive positioning, recent data suggests a more constructive backdrop is beginning to take shape. 

Rubner highlights that volatility has eased while capital flows are re-engaging, signaling renewed confidence among market participants. As earnings season gains momentum, investor focus is increasingly turning back to company-specific fundamentals, fostering a more selective, stock-driven environment. 

At the same time, a favorable supply-demand dynamic is developing. Institutional investors are adding risk exposure, systematic strategies are re-leveraging, and retail participation is picking up following the conclusion of tax season. Corporations have also re-entered the market through share buybacks, further supporting demand for equities.

Options market activity reinforces this shift. Institutional flows have turned decisively more constructive, with recent sessions tracking toward the strongest weekly net buying skew since October 2024. Notably, investors are extending positioning further along the curve, with increased demand for upside exposure in June and September expiries—particularly in single stocks—marking a move away from the short-term, tactical trading that dominated in March.
